patches.replace: 5.2% mean error (≈52 m per km, or ~520 km over a trans-hemispheric route)
patches.rationale: Arithmetic error: 5.2% of 1 km is 52 m not 520 m. Flagged by curmudgeon c3 as major finding that dome defenders will use to discredit the section.

patches.find: The dome found something real: projecting a sphere onto a flat disc creates systematic distance distortions. But the dome's best formula still has 21% unexplained error — while standard spherical geodesy achieves sub-meter accuracy. The distortion pattern matches azimuthal equidistant projection, which is a known map projection, not new physics.
patches.replace: The dome found something real: projecting a sphere onto a flat disc creates systematic distance distortions. The V50.4 quadratic WIN-027 presents was superseded by V50.6 H(r), which still needs four fitted parameters against 19 cherry-picked pairs — while standard spherical geodesy needs two parameters and achieves sub-meter precision globally. The distortion pattern matches the azimuthal equidistant projection, a known map projection, not new physics.
patches.rationale: TLDR claims dome best formula has 21% unexplained error, but dome superseded quadratic with V50.6 H(r) reporting 5.2% error. Casual readers get wrong picture of current dome state. Curmudgeon c3 flagged as moderate.

patches.find: 21% unexplained error is enormous — standard geodesy achieves sub-meter precision. The distortion pattern matches a known map projection, not dome geometry.
patches.replace: Both the V50.4 quadratic and its V50.6 H(r) replacement fail the same out-of-sample test: they are projection calibrations tuned on a small curated set of pairs. Standard spherical geodesy achieves sub-meter precision globally with two parameters; the dome needs four and still cannot handle trans-hemispheric routes.
patches.rationale: Mirror TLDR evidence fix — remove misleading 21% framing, replace with accurate characterization of both V50.4 and V50.6 failures.

patches.replace: (sub-millimetre absolute error on the full WGS84 ellipsoid, per Karney 2013 — accuracy does not degrade with distance)
patches.rationale: Vincenty/Karney error is an absolute bound, not a per-km rate. Framing as 0.5 mm per km invites defenders to ask if error accumulates at 10,000 km. Curmudgeon c3 flagged as moderate.

patches.find:
Self-contradiction: the dome's own refraction fix breaks this prediction. Section 1.8 documents how the dome's aetheric refraction correction — introduced to resolve the sun/firmament collision at 5,733 km — splits the sun's altitude into an "apparent" value (5,733 km, an optical illusion) and a "physical" value that must stay below the firmament ceiling H(r). But H(r) varies with orbital radius: ~3,618 km at the Tropic of Cancer and ~1,975 km at the Tropic of Capricorn. The physical sun altitude therefore oscillates by over 1,600 km across seasons — a swing the dome has never modelled. Eclipse shadow geometry requires a definite physical sun position to determine where the shadow falls and how it moves. Across 39 eclipses spanning 25 years and all seasons, the dome cannot specify the physical sun altitude for any of them without a seasonal model it does not have. This makes the ensemble prediction indeterminate: the dome cannot retrodict the shadow paths it claims to explain.

This is not an abstract concern. The dome's own 2026 eclipse predictions (E001–E009) use the formula ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF, where the Field Strength Factor (FSF) derives from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry — the same function whose seasonal indeterminacy Section 1.8 documents. The dome's future quantitative predictions are built on the function that makes its past claims indeterminate.

The dome's claimed mechanism is "aetheric field perturbation by geometric shadow blocking the field interaction between sun and dome" (raw-text/02-wins.txt). This is a physical field interaction: the shadow physically blocks an energy pathway. Unlike the standard photoionization mechanism (which tracks the apparent shadow path as seen from the ionosphere), a physical field blockage requires the physical sun position to determine shadow geometry. The refraction cascade directly undermines this: if the physical sun position is seasonally indeterminate, so is the physical shadow that supposedly blocks the aetheric field.
patches.replace: For a separate internal-consistency concern targeting the dome's own mechanism (the refraction cascade makes the physical sun position seasonally indeterminate across the 39-eclipse ensemble), see Section 1.8.
patches.rationale: Cascade paragraphs (Self-contradiction grade arguments) embedded inside Std Model Explains verdict creates hedge that dome defenders exploit. Curmudgeon c4 rated defense_survives=4. Demote to cross-reference per recommendation.
After removing cascade paragraphs, the P5 opening still references a self-contradiction that is now only a cross-reference. Fix to standalone sentence.

patches.find:
The formula is an internal model derivation: it is algebraically constructed from assumed dome parameters (cavity height, disc shape). The contrast with independent data is significant: INTERMAGNET observatory data will directly test the FSF formula's 9 station-specific predictions (E001–E009, projecting different magnetic anomaly amplitudes based on each station's position within dome geometry) when the August 12, 2026 eclipse occurs — but none of that observational data exists yet. It is not an independent measurement or constraint from outside the model. Deriving a mathematical relationship from assumed geometry and then testing it against measured data amounts to testing the model's internal consistency, not testing the model against nature.

Moreover, the eclipse predictions (E001–E009) that would test the FSF formula against independent INTERMAGNET data are all still pending — the August 12, 2026 eclipse has not yet occurred. Claiming a formula as a 'confirmed WIN' before the observations that would test it is circular: the formula is confirmed because it was derived, not because it was tested.

Claiming the FSF formula as a 'confirmed WIN' before a single per-station eclipse reading validates it is circular: the formula is confirmed because it was derived, not because it was tested. When August 12, 2026 eclipse data arrives from the 9 INTERMAGNET stations, the predictions become falsifiable — but until then, this WIN confirms nothing beyond internal algebraic consi
patches.replace:
The FSF formula's output is version-unstable: under V51 parameters (ellipse H(r), V51 station radii) the published values (EBR 1.029, HAD 1.471, LER 2.075, etc.) reproduce to within ~3%, but under V50.6 parameters (exponential H(r), V50.6 radii) the same formula produces HAD 1.89 — a 28% discrepancy at a single station. The BOU reference station that anchors the ratio moved from r = 5,715 km (V50.6) to r = 9,983 km (V51). A formula genuinely derived from V12 geometry should not swing 28% when the author revises other parameters; WIN-044's detail_claim asserts V12 derivation, but the reproduction profile shows the formula is tied to V51-specific parameters. A second structural contradiction is documented in Section 1.8: dome disc-radii are back-computed from Polaris elevation angles, making any formula that uses those radii — including FSF = (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) — parasitic on globe coordinates.

The eclipse predictions (E001–E009) that would independently test the FSF formula against INTERMAGNET station data cannot occur until the August 12, 2026 eclipse. Until then, this WIN confirms nothing beyond internal algebraic consistency — the formula is treated as 'confirmed' because it was derived from dome parameters, not because it was tested against nature.
patches.rationale: detail_evidence LEAD uses Misleading-era consistency-not-truth framing from c2 era. New verdict is Self-Contradicted but evidence still leads with old frame. Curmudgeon c4 rated defense_survives=4. Rewrite to lead with version-instability per recommendation.

patches.find: The 'derivation from V12 geometry' claim is therefore version-unstable.
